text_blocks.text: Prime numbers give rise to two
more general concepts that apply to
elements of any commutative ring R,
an algebraic structure where
addition, subtraction and
multiplication are defined: prime
elements and irreducible elements. An
element p of R is called prime
element if it is neither zero nor a
unit (i.e., does not have a
multiplicative inverse) and satisfies
the following requirement: given x
and y in R such that p divides the
product xy, then p divides x or y. An
element is irreducible if it is not a
unit and cannot be written as a
product of two ring elements that
are not units. In the ring Z of
integers, the set of prime elements
equals the set of irreducible elements,
which is
